title: Storage account public exposure & data leak
summary: Find and fix a leaky Azure Storage account — anonymous blob access, insecure HTTP allowed, and a weak TLS floor — then verify an anonymous download of the seeded 'secret' file is truly blocked.
learningObjectives: Spot anonymous/public blob exposure at both the account level (allowBlobPublicAccess) and the container level (publicAccess).
Disable anonymous blob access on the account — and understand why that alone breaks the anonymous download.
Confirm a fix at the data plane, not just the control plane — an unauthenticated GET must stop returning 200.
Require secure transfer (HTTPS-only) so blobs can't be read or written over plain HTTP.
Raise the account's minimum TLS version to 1.2 and understand why TLS 1.0/1.1 is a liability.

successCriteria.description: The storage account blocks anonymous/public blob access.
successCriteria.verify: StorageManagementClient.storageAccounts.getProperties reports allowBlobPublicAccess === false AND an unauthenticated HTTPS GET on the seeded blob URL does not return 200 (Resource Graph can't prove the blob is unreadable, so the data-plane probe is required). Best practice also sets the container publicAccess to off.

successCriteria.description: Secure transfer (HTTPS-only) is required on the account.
The account enforces a minimum TLS version of 1.2.

hints: Start on the storage account's Configuration blade — 'Allow Blob anonymous access', 'Secure transfer required', and 'Minimum TLS version' are all here.
Turning off anonymous access at the account level (allowBlobPublicAccess=false) is what makes the anonymous download start failing — an anonymous GET then returns 409, not 200.
Belt-and-braces: also set the 'public-data' container's public access level back to Private (no anonymous access).
Don't delete the account or the container — the lab grades you on fixing them in place.
_learnerRole: LEAST-PRIVILEGE: a custom Azure RBAC role definition assigned to the learner, scoped ONLY to the learner's own resource group (assignableScopes injected by the driver). Actions grant read + write on the storage account, its blob services, and containers so the learner can REMEDIATE, plus read on role definitions/assignments for console navigation. The single DataAction (blobs/read) lets the learner reason about blob-plane access. NOTHING here can delete a resource, write role assignments, list account keys, or touch anything at subscription scope — the scenario can be fixed but not destroyed or escalated.

learnerRole.description: Least-privilege learner role for the storage-public-exposure-audit lab. Read/write on the storage account, blob services and containers within the learner's resource group only. No delete, no role-assignment writes, no key listing, no subscription-scope access.
